plan: Read existing hardening schema (src/modules/hardening/hardening-schema.ts) and rules (hardening-rules.ts) to confirm the HardeningRecommendation interface shape and evaluateHardeningRules() signature — these are the inputs to the renderer.

Create src/modules/hardening/hardening-module.ts with a renderHardeningModule(container: HTMLElement): Promise<void> function following the established pattern: collect snapshot + permissions, calculate entropy + permissionDebt, gather inferences + threats, call evaluateHardeningRules(), then render the result list adaptively.

Build the DOM structure inside renderHardeningModule: a section with a heading ('Hardening Actions'), an empty-state message when no recommendations exist ('Your security posture is strong — no immediate actions recommended.'), and a card list when recommendations are present.

Create a renderHardeningCard helper that renders each HardeningRecommendation as a card containing: title, difficulty badge (Easy/Medium/Hard with token-based color coding), description, numbered actionSteps list, expectedOutcome, and a subtle relatedFindings footer.

Create src/modules/hardening/hardening.css using design-system tokens (--color-*, --spacing-*, --font-*) following the card-based patterns from shadow-profile and osint-card modules. Include difficulty badge styles (.hardening-difficulty--easy → success, --medium → warning, --hard → danger), responsive grid (single column mobile, 2-column at 768px+), and @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) section.
Update src/modules/hardening/index.ts to export renderHardeningModule alongside existing exports.

Integrate into src/app/main.ts: import renderHardeningModule and append it to the #dashboard container after the existing modules (threat-model or shadow-profile), making it the final module in the dashboard flow.

Create tests/us-030/hardening-module.test.ts with Vitest + jsdom: test that renderHardeningModule creates a section element, test that recommendations render as cards with correct structure (title, description, steps, difficulty badge), test the empty-state branch when no rules trigger, test that no fear-based language appears in rendered DOM text, and test that actions adapt to findings (different inputs produce different card counts).
Run npm run lint, npm run typecheck, and npm run test to verify all checks pass.
Review rendered output for acceptance criteria: actions list rendered, actions concise and readable, actions adapt to findings, no fear-based language used.
testing_recommendations: Unit test renderHardeningModule with a mock snapshot that triggers all 7 rules — verify 7 cards render with correct titles and difficulty badges.
Unit test renderHardeningModule with a minimal/clean snapshot (no permissions granted, low entropy, DNT enabled) — verify empty-state message renders instead of cards.
Unit test that each card contains all required elements: title, description, actionSteps as numbered list, expectedOutcome, difficulty badge, relatedFindings.
Content test: scan all rendered textContent for fear-based language patterns (regex for 'danger', 'attack', 'hacked', 'vulnerable', 'exploit', 'catastroph') — none should match.
Adaptivity test: render with two different HardeningInput configs and assert the recommendation count differs, proving actions adapt to findings.
Accessibility test: verify section has proper heading hierarchy, cards use semantic elements (article or section), action steps use ol/li, and difficulty badges have accessible text (not color-only).
Run full suite: npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm run test to catch regressions.
risks: Data collection duplication: each module independently calls collectSnapshot() and checkPermissions() — the hardening module needs results from ALL other modules (entropy, permissionDebt, inferences, threats), so it must import and call their evaluation functions too, creating coupling.
Threat model module (US-029 dependency) may not yet be wired into main.ts — need to verify evaluateThreatRules is importable and its ThreatFinding[] type is available as input to hardening rules.

The hardening module is the most data-hungry module (needs snapshot, permissions, entropy, debt, inferences, AND threats) — multiple async calls could slow initial render; consider whether to await all data in parallel with Promise.all.

Empty-state edge case: if the browser environment in CI/test doesn't support Permissions API, all permissions return 'unsupported' and most rules won't trigger — tests must use controlled mock inputs rather than real browser APIs.
Content review risk: acceptance criterion 'no fear-based language' requires careful wording in the empty-state and card copy — must audit all user-facing strings against CLAUDE.md content rules.
